text: A hiatal hernia is when the top of the stomach gets displaced or herniates through the diaphragm and up into the chest cavity. Although most people with a hiatal hernia do not experience any symptoms, others may suffer from recurrent symptomatic reflux requiring medication or even surgery. Surgical repair essentially involves pulling the stomach back into normal position fully below the diaphragm and making the opening in the diaphragm smaller. A mesh is then placed over the incision to reinforce the closure to minimize risk of hiatal hernia recurrence.

The surgical footage shows the dissection of the stomach, esophagus, and hernia sac from surrounding chest and abdominal soft tissue structures. The surgeon carefully identifies the vagal nerves to avoid injury during the mobilization of the hernia sac and esophagus. The esophagus is mobilized to ensure at least three centimeters of the distal esophagus lies within the abdomen, achieved through high mediastinal dissection.

The surgeon closes the enlarged diaphragmatic opening, or hiatus, with sutures to achieve a tension-free repair. A mesh is placed over the incision to reinforce the closure and minimize the risk of hiatal hernia recurrence.

A hiatal hernia repair is often performed in combination with some variation of fundoplication whether nissen or toupee to further control severe reflux. In some cases, hiatal hernia surgery can also be combined with weight loss surgery such as a sleeve gastrectomy.
